comments.en: Events in story plots: occurrences involving characters, places, and time. Includes an event hierarchy, participant roles, and temporal relations.

comment.en: A flowing watercourse.
Mortal living being, such as Men and Dwarces.
An interval with both start and end. Defined as StartingInterval ⊓ EndingInterval.
An interval with a known end.
An event in which a character is crowned or given authority.
An interval unbounded on at least one side.
A catastrophic natural event such as a flood, earthquake, or volcanic eruption.
A large settlement with political importance.
A hostile action directed at a patient. Left as a conservative subclass rather than a defined class, because patienthood alone would wrongly classify non-violent actions such as curses as attacks.
A marriage event with husband, wife, and officiant roles.
Moving from one place to another.
A single point in time.
Flat open land.
A chain of mountains.
A path or route connecting places.
A structural element that can contain other narrative parts.
A continuous extent of space. In story modelling, places occupy regions.
Events with physical consequences: battles, journeys, disasters.
The *Teryg (probably the plural form of Torog in Sindarin) are the Trolls, large humanoid monsters of great physical strength and weak intellect.
A main division of the narrative.
Events with social significance: births, deaths, marriages, coronations.
The only Vala who became evil. The enemy of all other Valar.

The evil beings are sentient agents that have chosen to do bad things, behave badly for the sake of doing bad, or became corrupted into doing evil. Some things are raised and bred to be evil. Some things are led to become evil by other evil beings by subversion. Some choose to become evil due to greed or as a way to overcome hardships of their lives.
The birth of a character.
The fictional story as a whole.
An atomic narrative element, like a single plot event.
A place on land.
A meeting involving verbal exchange.
A kingdom or administrative region containing settlements and wild areas.
A space inside a building or fortified complex.
An individual that does not die of natural death. Some immortals may die from wounds inflicted to them by violence or accident (like Elves), but some are truly eternal, like the Ainur).
Something that happens in the story world, with a time, place, and participants.
The Casari (plural form of Casar in Quenya), more often called with the Sindarin variant Naugrim (plural of Naug in Sindarin), are the Dwarves created by the Vala Aulë.
A body of water: river, lake, or sea.
A female individual. The gender may be a 'natural' one, attributed at birth, or a given one by social conventions.
Peredhil are descendent of both elves and humans.
The one God creator of everything.
A sperior entity. A singleton class that only contains Eru Ilúvatar, the One Allfather.
An object designed or used for combat. Example: Gurthang (Túrin's sword).

The Quendi (plural form of Quendë) are the first born of the Children of Ilúvatar, the Elves. They are named thus because they were the only ones who spoke when they first appeared, before Men and Dwarves came to be. They are more often called the Eldar.
Moving from one place to another.
Bigger than a hamlet, smaller than a city.
The death of a character.
An event with a deliberate agent. Defined as Action ≡ Event ⊓ ∃evt:hasAgent.⊤, so any event that has an agent gets classified as an action automatically.
A large body of salt water.
Placing a curse on someone. In the Narn, Morgoth curses the children of Húrin.
An interval with a known start.
A real-world person who contributes to the production of a narrative work.
A named location in the story world.
A male individual. The gender may be a 'natural' one, attributed at birth, or a given one by social conventions.
A crafted object of special significance, such as a jewel, ring, or magical item.
Superclass of instants and intervals.
The Ainur (plural form of Ainu) are the spirits created by Eru Ilúvatar before time existed
A state of ruling over a realm or people.
A real-world organization that publishes a narrative work.
A stretch of time between two instants.
A real-world person who originally wrote the work.
A means of transport used in a journey.
An inhabited place. Has a population.
The Atani (plural form of Atan) are the second born of the Children of Ilúvatar, the Men (i.e. Humans, not male people). They were given many names also by the Eldar.
The Maiar (plural form of Maia) are Ainur who entered Arda and serve the Valar.
A narrative element describing an in-world event. Bridges the plot layer and the event layer via describesEvent.
A structure with walls and a roof.
A prolonged conflict made up of multiple battles.
Uninhabited natural area.
A fortified settlement or stronghold, like Nargothrond or Menegroth. Not disjoint with City since some fortresses are city-sized.
A concluding section that follows the main narrative.
A very small settlement.
An ongoing state of affairs: a residence, captivity, or reign.
Two or more characters encounter each other.

The Edain (plural form of Adan) are the Men that belong to the Three Houses of Men that came to Beleriand in the elder days. Originally, “Adan” (plural “Edain”) is the Sindarin equivalent of Quenya “Atan” (plural “Atani”) but eventually came to mean this narrow subgroup.
Area covered mostly by trees.

The Eldar (plural form of Elda) are the Elves. They initially called themselves the Quendi, but the Vala Oromë, who met them at lake Cuiviénen under the stars, called them the Eldar. Now only the Quendi who followed Oromë to the West call themselves Eldar.
A state of living at a particular place for a period of time.
The greatest of the Ainur.
The Valar (plural form of Vala) are the most powerful Ainur who entered Arda, the world.
A violent confrontation.
Being held prisoner.
A large wooded area. E.g. Brethil in the Narn.
Deliberate speech: commands, declarations, curses, prophecies.
An action that involves an item. Any action with usesItem is classified here.
A real-world person who edited or compiled the published work.
A structural part of a story: prologue, chapter, or epilogue.
A real-world book, manuscript, or film script with authorship, editorial, and publication metadata.
Evil and powerful monster.
A significant object that can be used in events. Items include weapons, vehicles, and artifacts.
Standing water surrounded by land.
An introductory section that precedes the main narrative.

The Eruhíni (plural form of Eruhin), literally “the Children of Eru”, are usually called the “Children of Ilúvatar”. They are the Elves and Men. “Children of Ilúvatar” is used to collectively refer to the two races of Men and Elves, and the singular form is rarely used.
Event E1 ends exactly when event E2 starts.
A relation between someone and their parent
When an interval begins.
The character or entity holding the captive.
Inverse of hasSubEvent.
Inverse of fostersChild.
A story section contains plot events.
The first plot event in a story or section.
Allen's 'starts': same start time, first ends before second.
Relates someone to someone else that they serve. Relation servant-master.
Inverse of hasParticipant.
Allen's 'equals': same start and end.
The real-world date the narrative work was published.
The authority who validates the marriage (priest, king, etc.).
A relation between a mother and his child.
A female sibling of someone.
Proper part relation where the subject touches the boundary of the larger place, corresponding to RCC8 TPP at region level.
Strict ordering between instants.
A character involved in the event.
Inverse of isCapitalOf.
Enmity between two characters.
The destination place of a journey.
Inverse of occursBefore.
A male sibling of someone.
Inverse of TPP. First region contains the second, boundaries touching.
Characters who travel together or spend significant time together in the story.

Same spatial extent. Symmetric and transitive. Reflexivity is part of the intended RCC8 semantics but is not declared in OWL because ReflexiveProperty without domain/range would apply to all individuals, and with domain/range would force everything to be a Region.
The character held prisoner.
The winning side of a battle.
Allen's 'during': first interval is entirely within the second.
Allen's 'contains': inverse of during.
Two individuals who share the same parent.
Boundaries touch but interiors don't overlap.
A relation between someone and one their of descendants.
Top-level property grouping all social relations.
Alliance, cooperating towards a common goal. Not necessarily friends.
A character fighting in the battle.
Two distinct instants at the same point in time. Used when different IRIs represent the same moment.
An instant that falls within an interval.
Allen's 'started by': inverse of starts.
The two regions share no points at all.
Links a place to its spatial region. Functional (one region per place). Enables RCC8 reasoning on places.
Inverse of beforeEvent.
The character born.
When the event occurs (as an interval).
Links a plot event to the in-world event it describes.
Two places that share a boundary or are next to each other.
The publishing organization of the narrative work.
Allen's 'overlapped by': inverse of overlaps.
The title of a story, section, or narrative work. No domain restriction because both NarrativeContainer and NarrativeWork can have titles.
The original author of the narrative work.
The ordinal number of a chapter within the story.
A character undertaking the journey.
An editor or compiler responsible for the published work.
When an interval ends.
A textual summary. No domain restriction for the same reason as hasTitle.
The settlement is the capital of this realm. Functional and inverse-functional (one capital per realm, one realm per capital).
Allen's 'after': inverse of before.
The last plot event in a story or section.
One that is the child of a sibling of the parent of someone else.
Event E1 occurs entirely within the time span of event E2.
Allen's 'finishes': same end time, first starts after second.
The wife in the marriage.
A story is divided into sections.
Friendship between two characters.
Inverse of partOf.
The next section in the narrative. Derived from plot event ordering via property chain.
Allen's 'meets': first interval ends exactly when second starts.
When the event occurs (as an interval).
The next plot event in the narrative.
The character affected by the event.
The regions partially overlap. Each has parts not in the other.
A relation between a female child and his parent.
The character who dies.
First region is completely inside the second, boundaries don't touch.
Raising someone as a foster-child. E.g. Thingol fosters Túrin.
Inverse of serves. The subject has authority over the object.
The character residing at a place.
Inverse of hasSection.
E1 happens entirely before E2.
An event contains another as a sub-event. Transitive.
Allen's 'overlaps': first starts before second and ends during it.
Event E1 starts before and partially overlaps event E2.
Who deliberately performs the action. Sub-property of evt:hasAgent.
What happens as a consequence of the action, e.g. an attack results in a death.
Allen's 'before': first interval ends before second starts.
An event contains another as a sub-event. Transitive.
The place where an event occurs.
The mother. Range fo:Female.
Inverse of NTPP. First region completely contains the second.
A relation between a male child and his parent.
A vassal/servant relation. Linked to meo:served so facts work with both properties.
Allen's 'met by': inverse of meets.
When an interval begins.
When an interval ends.
A story section contains plot events.
Why the agent performed the action (textual description).
A story is divided into sections.
A character who speaks during the conversation.
The place where an event occurs.
A plot event earlier in the narrative. Transitive.
Allen's 'finished by': inverse of finishes.
People united through marriage.
Who is affected by the action.
Where a character has resided. No rdfs:domain declared because characters, peoples, and other agents could all live somewhere. Range is Place.
When the place was established. String because fictional dates don't always fit xsd:date.
X killed Y. Inferred from Death events.
First region is inside the second and their boundaries touch.
Inverse of hasTemporalExtent.
Proper part relation where the subject lies wholly inside the larger place, corresponding to RCC8 NTPP at region level.
Spatial containment between places. E.g. Menegroth is part of Doriath.
The husband in the marriage.
A relation between someone and one of their ancestors.
A relation between someone and one of their ancestors.
The father. Range fo:Male.
An item used in the event.
Number of inhabitants. Only makes sense for settlements.
A relation between a father and his child.
The active performer of the event.
A relation between a parent and their child
The starting place of a journey.
Who caused the death, if anyone.
Links the real-world work to the fictional story it tells.
A relation between someone and their parent
A relation between someone and their parent
